commentBody:
Acknowledging the Groundless Accusation 

I have drunk
your coffee
that was in the icebox

which was super 
duper
artisanal 
and special 

Forgive me
I had to try
because it was
so artisanal

(This one was my favorite of the funny themers in the puzzle!)

Nice, fun debut Colin! Some combinations of answers amused me for a variety of reasons: 

ARKS crossing with ASKS
SEITAN close to STANS
GODSPEED so soon after ARRIVE
DIPS under POOLSIDE (ITSATRAP!)

Congrats Colin!
Too easy for a Sunday.

With a few lucky guesses, it's done.  

You contrived a brutal debut, Collin Drown, a HANGTEN kind of ride, but well worth the trip(ups).  Thanks, and I'm sure we'll see you again.

Five puzzles and two loads of laundry.  Guess I'll call it a day.

Well I waited longer to word-check today. They were all correct, I finished but had one mistake I found with puzzle check. It was more fun waiting! That fighter pilots fight G FORCES and Lap is to REPASS. Good fun.
Eoin. Nope

This was fun, and definitely harder than usual. As a result, it lasted longer than usual, which extends the fun further.

On the other hand, there were an awful lot of clues that when I saw the answer I went "Huh? Oh, OK." I got them, but the distance between the clue and the answer felt too far to be fair.

I certainly appreciate a harder-than-usual Sunday puzzle, but for instance: "Noggin" for DOME just seems off. Noggin means head. Dome means the top of one's head, mainly if it's bald. Not the same. "Love seats" and SOFAS are both places for more than one person to sit. But I never heard anyone call a love seat a sofa before. "Put your nose where it doesn't belong" for MEDDLE also seems a bit off: Being nosy is not assaociated with *meddling* per se but rather with *prying * (being "nosy"). And I confess I don't know why DEMO is an "Audience stat" (but I'm sure someone will explain this).

On the positive side, the theme entries were all amusing, and I love the word AKIMBO. And I have spent many happy hours studying TORI, not just the usual tori that are homeomorphic to the surface of a bagel but also the n-dimensional tori (which are homeomorphic to the surface of an (n+1)-dimensional bagel).

Congratulations, Collin. Having a Sunday puzzle published on your first attempt is quite impressive. 

I like that the puzzle some bite (especially the FLEA crossing FLY section). 

Favorite clues [Device for sending group messages?] for OUIJA and [Bull rings?] for PRANK CALLS. 

[“It’s two-for-one night at the tequila bar”]

CHEAP SHOTS

(I hope no one takes the other kind)

I solved while watching the Norway vs. England match of the beautiful game, fantastically played by both teams.
So, first and foremost, congrats to our English friends in this forum for the win! Here's hoping this is the Cup you take home!

Also congrats to Collin who has given us an exciting debut, and an imaginative one. To me, the punning was subtle, which brought an elegance to the puzzle that I very much appreciated.

My favorite was BACKHANDED COMPLIMENT exactly because it's something I disdain. I call it "a kiss on one cheek and a slap on the other." A waste of a good opportunity to keep quiet. 

I'm guilty as charged of BAreFACEDLIE before the BALDFACED one, and I have no idea why I use one over the other. 

Thank you for a great puzzle, Collin, and please know that my compliments are sincere!

Did this puzzle while on a subway train in NYC. Definitely had to 102D quite a few times.

No matter how many times I visit, I'll never get used to the sticky heat in the tunnels.

I was stuck for the longest time because I had thought the answer was CRANK _____
until the light suddenly came on.  Great Sunday puzzle.  Don’t expect many CROSS WORDS about it.
I’m gonna be that person and point out that the singular of TAMALES is TAMAL. Otherwise great puzzle!

Congrats on your debut, Collin! A fun romp and some challenging answers for me (e.g., EOIN). Yikes. But I enjoyed the wordplay. Been doing the NYT puzzle for 50+ years. Just wanted to applaud your efforts!
No slights to your adjustments, Collin. Fine wordplay today. I hope your mom likes this one. For your next effort, go for those complex construction hijinx you love.

Fun one! To me, the puns were funny without being groaners. But I do like a good pun or five. Each one went in so fast for me with few crosses. Right up my alley!  Lots of fun! Felt like good, fresh fill to me too. Many chuckles out loud!

I finished 62% faster than average but it was a good night for a fast one....  Apparently, salmon doesn't cook itself. Har!  

Now it's time to feast on it. ☺️
Fun one! congrats on the debut.

Congrats on your debut! Very punny. I groaned a bit with the first one, BITING REMARK, because that seemed a little trite, and I would have been disappointed if they were all that cliche, but most of the rest of them were very fresh and clever. And long, too, which makes for a challenge especially for a debut constructor.

I was surprised some folks thought this was particularly difficult. I guess the phenomenon that I am seeing is that even though there may be several challenging entries, I still feel like there a lot of easy crosses on Sundays to offset them. So I, at least, found little resistance even though there were many thought provoking entries and clues.

I thought “bull rings?” was the star today. I was proud to get “Apple consumer” immediately, and was on the right track for “Turkey part” but was looking for EUROPE.

I would nit about HAZMATSUIT, I expect most workers in Hazmat suits are doing exactly what they want—microbiologists, radiation experts, etc. —and enjoy their job quite a bit. I guess people wearing them for environmental disasters, though, may not enjoy what they are doing, or at least if they do, wish it was a job that didn’t need to be done. Still a clever clue though!

Anyway, an excellent, well-crafted, and enjoyable debut, thanks Collin!
Awesome debut, congrats!

[That s=x worker must not want my business] 
AN UNSOLICITED COMMENT 

[I know we only met five minutes ago, but I totally love you!] PREMATURE EJ@CULATION 

[In answer to your question, this puzzle took me 24 minutes and 28 seconds] 
A MEASURED RESPONSE 

[You really wrapped my birthday present well!] 
OPENING STATEMENT

Random thoughts 
Happiness is no typos in the Sunday puzzle; therefore no fly specking,  though perhaps we can call it FLEA specking today.
Is it possible to HANG TEN on a OUIJA BOARD?
Idris ELBA in the lead off corner and Jamie Hector (Marlo) on the main Times site.  Turns out he's a Brooklyn guy, doing good for kids among all the other things he's got going on. Maybe it's time for a rewatch. 
All the themers are fun, but in honor of the Wimbledon finals Sunday, I'll go with BACKHANDED COMPLIMENT as my fave. May whoever has the best hair day win. 
Oh yeah, Dominic West was at Wimbledon the other day, looking very dapper in a creamy white (ercu?) three piece suit. Another sign. Definitely time for a rewatch.
Congratulations on your debut puzzle, Collin.

Breezy fun Sunday puzzle! Love the Punny theme

Rating:
9/10

87A ["Wow! With form like that, you're headed to Wimbledon!"] BACKHANDEDCOMPLIMENT

The clue goes great with the answer, but I always thought the phrase was "left-handed compliment" which I think is more elegant.

Maybe not.

More importantly, have you noticed that Jimmy Kimmel is bringing back the show "Who Wants To Be A Millionaire" on July 22?  The first team is Jamie Ding and Ben Affleck.

It's on my calendar. I can't wait. What fun!
easy (my best ever sunday time by 14 minutes) but still clever and fun.

Good, fun puzzle.  

You do nice work, Collin.

Good luck at VCU.

It's always appreciated (at least by me) when answers involve puns. Most of the themers came rapidly, but there were a couple of spots elsewhere that took crosses and a little thought. A nice, gentle Sunday puzzle, and a very nice debut.

Thanks, Collin!
Great debut, Collin!  Fun, engaging puzzle and loved the “slight” twist. Your mom must be bursting with pride.  Looking forward to the next one.

Most of you may know this, but the Big Crunch (114A) would theoretically be when the current expansion of the universe slows to a stop, then collapses back in to reform the Big Bang.

The tricky part of this concept is that it's easy to imagine galaxies and such spreading out, moving farther than father into already existing "empty" space.  But that's wrong.

Space is not empty.  It's full of particles that blink into and out of existence.  The law of Conservation of Matter and Energy doesn't apply because these particles last such a tiny fraction of time that it's jumbled into the Uncertainty Principle of Quantum Mechanics.

Moreover, space can expand *faster* than the speed of light.  The speed of light is the upper limit for a particle or wave moving through space.  When space itself being created, space can expand faster that even light can move through it.

Because of this, there is only so much universe that we can access.  Much (most?) of it is inaccessible through any means available.  Even if we had a spaceship that could move the speed of light, some parts of the galaxy would still outrun us.

Another possibility for the end of the universe is "The Big Rip".  In this theory, dark energy causes such an torrent of space creation that molecules, atoms, and the particle within atoms (nuclei, quarks, etc) are separated from one another, and fly away from one another forever.

I love cosmology, even if I don't get it completely.
Turkiye. Just saying not spraying. And lap is not a repass as one's already in front if one laps another competitor or racer on the track.
I also like PETSPA and the unusual cluing for OREO. And thankyou, HeathieJ, for explaining DEMO.
Pleases forgive the typos below. ("Like" should have been "liked" and thankyou is obvious.) Thanks.
Puns can be fun but overall it was kinda boring and pretty easy
32A would lose its challenge if the clue used the now preferred form Türkiye, with or without the umlaut, for the country’s name.
Easy breezy

My least favorite kind of theme - a set of puns so weak they make a wet noodle seem strong as an ox 🤷🏽. I solved the top third of the grid and didn't feel like continuing.

One thing I found interesting though before I quit - I did not know "to LARD" meant to embellish with unnecessary words. In Polish, the term is "lać wodę", "to pour water." Water and lard are nothing alike, are they? There are more such cases. For example, your "to jump out of the frying pan into the fire" is our "wpaść z deszczu pod rynnę".
My mother and I had a blast working through this punny puzzle. Absolutely brilliant.
Just last month, my streak was at 938 or so. Then, around 10 days ago, it changed to around 125. How can that be possible?

Defeated by the US spelling of PATRONIsING and my inability to spell GALOOT correctly. Gave up after some flyspecking. 

Never mind … the rest was a good Sunday puzzle.

"Who just puts a pencil back without sharpening it?"

POINTED QUESTION

“Your service was Clouseau-ian, and my tip is Scrooge-ian.”

GRATUITOUS INSULT
In keeping with yesterday's discussion, I guess IAGO was an antipopeye.
I just saw 97A [Planner unit], which I misread briefly as Platner unit. By my calculation, a Platner unit equals approximately two Friedman units.

Congratulations on your debut, Collin.  It was straightforward without being boring.  

And that's not a BACK HANDED COMPLIMENT.  I'm sure it's not easy to create an accessible but fun Sunday grid.  You ACED it!

Now that you've created one for your mom, I look forward to your next more diabolical construction.  I hope you already have one in the works.

Well, now, there’s an original theme, with the answers being types of negative things people say, and the clues punning on them.  It’s a left-field, one-of-a-kind theme.

And why not even make it more novel by having five of the six theme answers be debuts, never seen before in the 80+ years of the Times puzzle?  And that sixth answer – BALD FACED LIE – is a once-before.

Standing-O to Colin for coming up with this. 

Kudos also for lacing the box with wordplay in theme and non-theme clues.  I especially liked [Bull rings?], which brought a big “Hah!”, and [You might need to lose a few to get them] a riddle my brain loved trying to crack, even though it eventually needed crosses.

And then there was [Turkey part], for which I confidently slapped down WATTLE – “Hah!” again.

Your puzzle, Colin, was something different and something enjoyable, for which I’m most grateful.  Congratulations on a very promising NYT debut puzzle!
Brilliant! All the theme answers are great. Best/tightest Sunday in a LONG time.

EASY MODE, indeed.

But so many clever clues, who cares?  Especially like bull rings.

I loved figuring out this puzzle. And each theme was a new challenge with great rewards. Amazing debut! I had most trouble in the Mideast (sounds true to life!) as I had company instead of comment before getting the theme. Also, I had innard instead of Ankara for Turkey part. I had it working for me until I had to put in Hazmat suit.
Not to be the "well ackshually" guy but... 101 down is near the Olympic Park but not in it.

Flew through this 
then flyspecked for 15 minutes.

[Baking recipe instruction]
KNEAD It

[The Big Crunch, theoretically…]

EtD.

Thought it was the most clever 
way I’d ever seen ETD clued.

Congrats on your debut Collin!
I had DRUNK CALLS for 66D, thinking of people who dring Red Bull with vodka and then proceed to make phone calls that they might not have made while sober... :-)
Ya done real good Collin. Hopefully we'll see you here again.
The singular for “tamales” is “tamal” not “tamale”

A very fine Sunday puzzle, and an outstanding debut. Felt like the editorial team did justice to this nice grid and cute theme by keeping the clues appropriately puzzling. No assistance needed, but took me almost 40 minutes. Congrats to all!

I found the theme cute and enjoyable, but I have some nits.  Re the theme answers:  If you are complimenting someone on their backhand, then it's a BACKHAND COMPLIMENT, not a BACKHANDED COMPLIMENT.  All the other theme answers work, my favorite being GROUNDLESS ACCUSATION.

But what can you say about the ridiculous combination of letters that forms the name EOIN?  And what can you say about crossing it with a sci fi movie?  That "E" could be any letter at all.  You might say that TENET is the only actual word that fits there, but I protest.  If you don't know the movie, the answer might be TAN ET, for example.  An extraterrestrial that comes from a very sunny planet.  Or it might be TV NET -- an extraterrestrial colonizing both your television and your Internet.  Or it might be TM NET -- an extraterrestrial trademarking the Internet for his faraway planet.  You never know what calamity a sci fi movie might portend.  A really awful Natick, I thought.

Also, maybe someone will tell me why DEMO is an "audience stat"?  Or what a shop VAC is?

Nevertheless, my overall emotion about this puzzle was enjoyment.  The puns were fun.

Nice Sunday puzzle. Theme clues were in the vicinity of dad jokes, so should have been right up my alley. But... just took a lot of working the crosses for things to dawn on me. Just ended up being an enjoyable workout. And... of course a puzzle find today: A Monday from April 18, 2011 by James Tuttle. Four straightforwardly clued theme answers: LOVETRIANGLE FIFTEENMILES THIRTYSOMETHING FORTYTHIEVES And then finally... "Tennis winner's declaration" GAMESETMATCH No specific identification of that as the 'reveal' but thought that was just one really clever set of answers. Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=4/18/2011&h=57a .....
RVA ALL DAY good luck future Ram! Richmond is a great place to be a student.
Fun puzzle!

Sci-fi movie crossing some author's first name?
Obsolete (?) device crossing an exercise plus ?"Holesome" figures...

So, three letters missing/in error.
I got all of the Themers (some without help from the clues)...

White Claws?  DEMO is an audience stat?

Congrats.  sorta.

Congrats on your debut! A thoroughly enjoyable puzzle. I especially appreciated seeing Eoin Colfer’s name. I absolutely loved Artemis Fowl as a child and it was a joy to share the books with my daughter, who loved them as well.
Not sure I'd call the aral sea rejuvenated...

Fun puzzle! I got a little stuck in the lower left corner but overall it was a really fun solve, not too difficult and not too easy. For me, a few things I didn’t know but were easy enough to deduce from the crossings. I’m always amazed about the grouches and nitpickers here, or shout “Natick!” about any crossing they didn’t know. Great debut!!

I didn’t get that the themers were all “slights” of some kind until I read the column. Thanks for that, Caitlin.  

I really liked the long themers and thought the clues for each were cleverly worded. It takes a special mind to see how each slight could also represent certain situations. Well done, Collin!
I enjoyed doing your puzzle today.
someone was puzzled by SHOP VAC so I'll let Jonathan Coulton explain it to you in song. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xmC9PHx19Ac
Great puzzle! A lot of fun, very clever theme and clueing!
62A and 58D is a perfect example of creating a natick when there was absolutely no need to. Poor editing.

I love a good pun 😊. Heck, I love a bad pun 😉. So 👍👍 for this puzzle.  There were several crossings I was uncertain about so it was a pleasant surprise when my last entry gave the happy music (technically just the happy flash screen, as I have the music turned off). Flyspecking a Sunday can be so tedious. As someone who fat-fingers constantly, I am much too familiar with that experience 😭
Clean and entertaining, indeed! This was a lot of fun. :-)

And my usual late puzzle find. Just thought this was kind of a cute theme. Four theme answers, all straightforwardly clued: ARMSAKIMBO LEGSDIAMOND FOOTTHEBILL TOETHELINE Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=9/19/1989&h=17a ...
Could someone please explain the answer for "holesome" figures?

I am not usually a pun lover, but I laughed rather than groaned at these. Especially the negative phrases that were twisted into something positive. I liked that all of them were a bit "off." 

Tried to fit GROUND ESPRESSO into the coffee clue. 

The puzzle fell at a steady lope, and presently I found myself done except for the cross at _LINE and _EMO. Not really a fan of the gridiron, I did know of oLINE and saw that a D would make the down into something sensible although I had no idea how it fit the clue. 

Instead of entering that D right then, I decided to wander back through the grid. That provided a satisfying amount of puzzle time and a satisfying conclusion when I did add the D. 

Like others I found this a gentle, and gently engrossing, Sunday solve.

Cute, but not a Sunday. More like what used to be a Tuesday or Wednesday, expanded for a larger grid. 

I've been doing a lot of the archives, and historically - until pretty recently - there was a clear progression: Tuesdays were actually a bit tough. Wednesdays were actually challenging. Thursdays were difficult with a rebus. Fridays were generally punny with white space. Saturdays were a lot of white space and hard. 

Sundays were the challenging and clever pieces de la resistance that I waited for all week and could settle down with a cuppa and pore over for a good hour. It was such a treat!

Over the past few years everything has blended into a mishmash of cutesy pieces that may be randomly challenging but otherwise have no rhyme nor reason. It is neither disrespectful nor condescending to call this out. It is reality. 

What's frustrating is that comments in this context are too often taken as personal criticisms of the puzzle author or solvers, rather than what they actually are - constructive feedback and a desperate plea for the editors. 

Just maybe, some day, a new editor will actually take the time to go back and try a Tuesday or Wednesday crossword circa 2019, review the history, and think to themselves "Wow! I get it!" And bring back the real NYT crossword.
Who'd a thunk that there were 3 palindromic relatives?  SIS jumped right out but I had to dig a little for DAD (MOM next time?)
Horrendous
I read every comment but didn’t find what I was looking for: how does one lose a few ABS to get ABS?
Amazingly fun Sunday crossword, thank you! Only one thing puzzled me (apologies for the pun), why 23A had the hint with "vant" instead of "want"? I vant to knov!

I really wanted the answer to “Baker’s necessity” (38D) to be KNEAD. I just hate to waste a good (or bad— really, usually bad) pun.

Always happy to see an inventive way to clue OREO.  And speaking of vowel-heavy words, I’m surprised we don’t see IAGO more often. Or OUIJA, for that matter. Maybe it’s that pesky “J”. 

For anyone who hasn’t seen In BRUGES, I recommend you rectify the situation immediately. I love that movie.

Aah, time for lunch. I think I’ll have a TAMALE.

Bit late today, blaming it on the v late night last night as England forced us to watch extra time between our fingers at silly O’clock this morning. Got the result we wanted though so yay, onto the semis.

Good puzzle. Though not generally a pun fan these were cute and raised a chuckle or two. BITING REMARK especially, as I’m currently reading a Vampire/Witch Romantasy. Don’t judge me, it’s for our book club.

84A is the name of the resident Shiba. We visited the city some years ago while visiting No1 son. The beef is indeed glorious, though so expensive we literally had one bite each.

While I’ve seen this as a clue/answer before, I finally looked up SELTZER. It’s not something you generally come across here, certainly not in the pubs anyway, though Google assures me it’s available in the UK. Sounds like what we call flavoured sparkling water perhaps? Not a fan myself; the ‘fruit’ flavours leave a nasty taste. Talking of, on a US based cruise a while back I ordered a Diet Coke. I don’t know what they do to that drink in the US. It did NOT taste good; super sweet with a chemical aftertaste that made my teeth curl.

Could anyone explain shop/VAC pls? I can’t work it out at all.

The clueing here is something I found clicked with my sense of humor right away. Besides the puns, I noticed a few palindromic answers, references to some technology I barely remember using as a child, and one of the most innovative clues (for OREO) I've seen yet for a crossword staple. Two thumbs up, I like when a puzzle puts a smile on my face. Happy Sunday and happy solving!

It’s 2:25pm eastern. Only about 200 comments, so I am guessing there are no big controversies today. 

Posting before I read. 

This wasn’t difficult, but for whatever reason, things didn’t come to me quickly. The themers were actually the easiest part for me. Sometimes. It just doesn’t click. 

Got the gold.  But feel like I should have definitely gotten through this easier 

Favorite clue was “far from basic” for ACIDIC. 

I got no clue who this EOIN Colf ET dude is, but I’m don’t feel like I’ve seen that entry before. And there is no way,  a name like that shouldn’t be glue in every crossword made every single day.  

I was sure that was my mistake on the “something’s not quite tight” message. But I just fat fingered a random entry somewhere else. 

A think a standard Sunday?  Maybe that sounds like shade. But it isn’t meant to be.

Enjoyed this one.  Learned a few things, like seitan.  May have to try it.  About my average, which is way long, but no lookups or hints.  Had to settle for a blue since I had one letter off:  figured I would knead it rather than knead in  while baking; and as far as I know an etd could be the big crunch.  Oh well.

Back in the crossword saddle after 3 weeks off.
Excellent Sunday puzzle, thanks, Collin.

I do call a personal Natick on 58D/62A, T_NET / _OIN. Names can be spelled any which way, and sci-fi titles could be any conglomeration of letters.

A lot of slights being bandied about in today's news.
Can someone explain TORI to me?
LEFTHANDEDCOMPLIMENT before BACKHANDED. . . They both fit the clue! Anyone else do that?

32A:  Turkey Part
I already had the ANK___ and wrote in ANKLES.
Hmmmm, I thought, "Do turkeys have ankles?"
Got the trick at 23 across pretty quickly but took a long time to parse out the other theme answers. Fun, chewy Sunday puzzle!
Enjoyed this a lot!
Lap = repass?
The singular of "tamales" is not "tamale" it's "tamal." Slight annoyance.
The themed answers were pretty easy in my opinion and the fill wasn't too difficult, either. I finished about 30 seconds of my Sunday PR for a time of 12:23.

“Your service will be reflected in your paltry tip.”


GRATUITOUS INSULT
This puzzle provided a small log-jam around a certain misdirecting clue that I loved a little bit. That’s it for my ‘dam with feint’ praise.

I apologize, friends, for being so verbose, but this puzzle brought out some vivid experiences.

Shop-vac.

There was recently a flood in my basement when the sump pump failed during a very rainy spell.  I shop vaced the water up, and then I realized I was downstairs, and I needed to get this very heavy mass of water out.

But I could only lift it a couple of inches.  

I tried to muscle it up the basement stairs, but it was slow going, a step at a time.  

Half way up I was in trouble.  I was getting weaker, and every time I picked up the shop vac to move it up a step, I teetered on falling backwards down the stairs.  But I was halfway up.  Going back would have been just as hard.

My bad lower back was also killing me, but mostly I thought of falling backwards onto the concrete floor.  (I was alone and this was the middle of the night).  

I finally got it to the top stair, my heart racing at a terrifying rate from effort and terror.

I called my wife, woke her up, thinking I needed to tell her my close call.  "Why didn't you just use a bucket to transfer water from the shop vac to the downstairs drain, until you could lift it?"

Some times I'm just astonished about how I can talk reasonable well about dark energy can still be so stoopid.

Will Shortz thinks, "Some lively, lovely non-theme answers appear at 29- and 98-Across, and 14- and 66-Down.”

Huh, nothing lively or lovely to me. Meh.

My favorite OREO clue thus far 😻
Congrats on a fine debut!

And yet one more puzzle. Probably re-post this tomorrow. A Sunday from October 15, 2014 by Bill Zais with the title: "Halloween Costumes." Some theme answers: WEREWOLFBLITZER TOMBSTONEPHILLIPS EYEOFNEWTGINGRICH GRAVEDIGGERPHELPS GHOSTBUSTERKEATON BLACKCATSTEVENS Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=10/25/2015&h=122a ....

GROUNDLESS ACCUSATION made me think back to the groan worthy days of old TV ads like the one where the husband would tell his wife "Honey, this coffee is grounds for divorce". And men being randomly interviewed on the street and asked "Would you say your wife's pie crust is flaky?" One of those ads had a guy go to a nearby phone booth (which Clark Kent wasn't using at the time) and call his wife to talk about it. Times have changed.
I really enjoyed this one. Nothing too obscure. Something time and patience can solve. And the theme was fun. Well done!
Sorry but this was one of the most boring puzzles I've done in a while.  Just dull.  I finished it, correctly,  without help, but it was a slog.

Just finished now (almost midnight on Sunday). Teeth-grindingly hard with many razes and rebuilds of various blocs. Looking forward to reading the chat. Over 1:15 for me, of which the last ten minutes were speckling for what I was sure was the trouble area. You know how this ends, right? Typo was absolutely elsewhere.
Still no consults. All My Own Work.

@Cat Lady Margaret

Weren’t they plums?

@Cat Lady Margaret
In college I lived in a huge house  with 20-30 fellow students. we had two shared fridges and people were always complaining that someone was stealing their food. Someone bought a half-gallon of fresh-squeezed OJ and wrote on the package "I spat in this OJ".  By morning someone had added "So did I".

@Steve L

And they were delicious!
@Cat Lady Margaret my partner, the professional poet (seriously), says give that lady 19 stars.

@Steve L
People who demand "the pitiless beat of the metrical feet" get quite annoyed by this poem.

@Steve

Whoops, it’s Collin, my mistake! Congrats Collin!!
@Mimi Metallurgicowell Im in here looking for hints!  I guess everyone experiences puzzles differently. I enjoyed figuring out the clever responses. Now I just need to find my mistake!

@Mimi Metallurgico
nah. yes at present xwstats has it as easy, but Sundays are supposed to be large Wednesdays, and I'd say this fit the bill.
@Convoid-04 And Collin what a cool thing to be studying! Godspeed.

@George
uh... yup. eleven (and counting) very popular books. And that's how he spells his name. I wasn't familiar with it, but it's fair and apparently pretty common in Ireland.  FYI TIL it's pronounced like "Owen".
@George It was a fair answer. I'm 72 and had no problem with it. He's been popular for quite a while and I knew him because my now 29-year-old offspring had one of his books 20 years ago.

George,
What exactly do you find "nope" about EOIN?

@George

We can only hope that he registers your disapproval and changes his name immediately.
@Ed My now 28 year old offspring had all of them (a bit of a completist). We even went to an event where he was speaking. He was hilarious!

@Dan

DEMOgraphic....  Stats about the demographics of the people watching.

HeathieJ, thanks for explaining. But now that I understand it, I can add  "Audience stat" for DEMO for as yet another clue/answer combo that strikes me as off. One often hears about "demographics". As someone who has worked in statistics, however, I have never heard of just one audience "demographic".

Dan,
What is the demographic for the audience for this show?

@Dan

I had a four dimensional bagel the other day.  

It kept falling out of my stomach through that extra dimension..

@Beverly

You are too kind.

Even such simple sounding tasks as defining exactly what we mean by "universe" can be troublesome.

To the extent that I understand it, which is purely as a hobbyist, we might talk about the universe being "everything" (although we might have trouble defining "everything", too)

Or we could say that the universe is the part of that whole which is accessible to us.  In this sense any portion of the whole universe that is beyond what we could reach even at light speeds, is really "beyond" our "observable" universe.  

One key thing I should emphasize,  if we can't reach them even at light speeds, they can't reach us either.  So in a sense we're in separate universes.  Unreachable to each other.

Then there is the multiverse theory, for another day.

As always, I'd love for someone who has greater insight into things like this to chime in with corrections or comments.

@Your Fired I think REPASS is dreadful fill, but to your complaint… if you were never behind another participant and you lap them, you would be passing them for the first time. However, If at any point you were behind them, you would pass them and then when you lap them you would re-pass them. Or if you lap them again, you would re-pass them. So the “maybe” in the clue is doing some work.

Nothing wrong with the clue, but IMO, this kind of fill should be avoided.

@Your Fired @Jeb
Ah the perennial "is it OK to make a new word starting with 'Re' " argument. My parents used to play a game called Anagrams.  It involved rolling "dice" with letters. One could take an opponent's word, add letters and make a new word. My father converted my mother's NICKEL into RENICKEL and was prepared to die on that hill (you can REPLATE and REGILD, can't you?) but neither the dictionary nor my mother was buying it.  It became a family joke.
 Actually, I believe I've told this story here before, but surely I may RETELL it.
  With parents like that, how could I not grow up as a word-nerd?

Plain old seltzer water is not flavored. Flavors can be added to it and those beverages can be sweetened. I just enjoy Seltzer on its own if im bored with still water and if feeling festive add a lime wedge.

@Helen Wright
Congratulations on your nation's progress. I'm rooting for Albion!
On the seltzer front: if you asked me, I could write a book.  Oh, you didn't ask? Settle in anyway.
In the tenements of the Lower East Side, seltzer was both employment and remedy. Hauling glass bottles up a five-storey walkup was no walk in the park but you could earn a living. And the seltzer was always /safe/ water, no small matter when plumbing was unreliable – even in NYC which would eventually become renowned for its water quality. 
Til today, there are family names like Seltzer and Seltzerman. If you're a fan of the Marx Bros., watch how much use Harpo gets out of a seltzer bottle. 
Sadly, I don't know the brand named here. We get White Rock from the US but since I'm currently trying to avoid US products, I'm not buying it either.
As for Diet Coke: see next post.

@Helen Wright
As to Diet Coke: it seems they learned nothing from the New Coke debacle, a mistake so huge and predictable that it is taught in business schools as a cautionary tale. I'm currently buying Diet Coke since Pepsi Zero (which I had been drinking for a while) introduced ginseng (?%$*/?!?) of all things into its formula. 
All these sodas have extremely localized variants (e.g., it is widely known that Québec prefers its sodas sweeter than anglo-Canada) which are constantly being jimmied. So your onboard Diet Coke may have been the formulation preferred by the ship's home port, its last port of call, its wholesale supplier's base city, or even the cheapest available version. (Sorry.)
As others have said, pure seltzer is plain, no flavouring. I find most fruit juices too thick and sweet for my taste but I really enjoy soda and apple juice, grape juice, or no-pulp OJ mixed half-and-half or even 2-1 in favour of soda.

@Barry Ancona

You're right--my story has HOLES.  

The floor drain is a cut through the concrete right to the ground below (I think).  When the water table rises above that, I rely on the sump pump to get rid of it.  So effectively the water was coming from the floor drain.

The shop vac had probably about 10 or 15 gallons of water in it, and I couldn't lift it all to the sink, about four feet off the ground.

But, to your point that there had to be a safer, easier way, yes, you are 100% right.
